text: The message was short, and not the way she wrote when something could wait.
She needed him on a call, if the link held. She had a notice. It was bad.
He pulled the cracked shop screen around on its arm and propped the phone into the cradle that fed the relay.
Can you hear me.
Most of it.
I'm going to read you something.
Under the thin voice was the quiet she got when she was angriest, the quiet other people mistook for calm.

Came an hour ago. Formal notice. From the manufacturer. Three systems. The diagnostic scanner. The medication-management unit. And the respiratory controller. All three lose remote authentication at midnight tonight.
Eli closed his eyes a second.
Read me the wording.
Following a review of supported-region status, remote authentication for the listed devices will be discontinued effective end of day, October the third. We appreciate your continued cooperation.

The same flat thank-you as the doorbell and the dairy case, applied now to a scanner that found what was wrong inside a person, a unit that decided how much of a drug went into a body, and a controller that kept a set of lungs breathing that could not do the work on their own.
All right.
He reached for the bench, for the steadiness of it, because working a problem through, step by step, was the only ground he knew how to stand on.
Tell me what stops. Physically. At midnight, when the authentication goes.

The scanner won't boot. It checks a license at startup; the server stops answering, it stops booting. The med unit, I don't know yet. It might keep the doses already loaded and refuse new ones, or just lock. The respiratory controller is the one that frightens me. It runs continuous. Phones home on a cycle to confirm it's authorized. If it can't confirm, the safest thing they could have programmed is keep running and complain. The likeliest thing they actually programmed is degrade, or stop. I won't know which until midnight, and I am not going to find out at midnight with somebody on it.
Who's on it.
A silence that was not the link.

There's a man. Sixty-one. He comes off it in the day, mostly, but he can't hold a night without it yet. There's a child some nights. Not tonight. But some nights. I'm not giving you their names, Eli. That part isn't mine to give. But I'm not handing you three machines and a deadline either, because the people on them are why I'm calling, instead of just unplugging the things and going home.
I know.
Do you.
No cruelty in it. A real question, and she waited for a real answer.

I can hear you doing it. You've got it on a bench in your head. Scanner, med unit, controller. Three problems, sorting them by how hard they are. It's not three problems. It's one decision somebody made in a building, and the bottom of it is a man who can't breathe through the night. Don't climb back up onto that bench, where it's clean and it's just machines and nobody's dying. Stay down here with me. Where the man is.

He did not answer right away. He looked at the three machines sitting in plain type with a single time beside them, 23:59, three people and one clock, and the cold came up through the floor of the shop and into him, more of it than the room could account for.

It's the same thing, Lena. The towers this morning. The grocery. They dropped us a tier at noon. No more emergencies. It's not three companies having a bad week. It's everyone, at once, deciding we're not worth it. Your clinic is just where it shows up with a body underneath.
I know what it is. I've known what it is for a year. I'm asking you what we do about the man tonight.

And here was the place he had been walking toward all morning, without letting himself see it. Because he could see the fix. That was the worst of it. He could see exactly the move that had saved the doorbell and the thermostat and the router, the whole of what he did. When a server stopped answering and left a machine stranded, you stood in for the server. You forged the permission it could no longer grant, and the machine, not knowing the difference, ran. A withheld yes and a vanished yes were the same silence to the device. He could counterfeit a yes a hundred times over before midnight.
And under it, the thing he could not say to her.

The yes on that controller was vouching for something. The calibration. The dosing. The pressure that holds a body inside it. I can forge the pointer by hand. I cannot forge what it points at. Not in the hours I have. Not ever.

That was the wall. The doorbell's permission had stood in front of a chime; cut it loose and the worst it did was ring at the wrong time. The controller's permission stood in front of a man's lungs. Cut that loose on a hand-made yes, and the controller would keep running and kill the man it was keeping alive, slowly, correctly, reporting the whole time that everything was fine.

And it was not one controller. Behind the man, a clinic. Behind the clinic, a neighborhood, every machine in it orphaned the same way, every one needing its own forged yes and its own certainty he could not forge. No bottom to it. Only him, with a screwdriver, one device at a time, while the whole of it kept coming.
Eli. You went quiet. Tell me you didn't go quiet because there's nothing.
I'm here. I'm thinking.

It was true, and it was not enough, and they both knew it. He told her he would come, he would bring what he had, he would look at the controller with his own eyes before he said anything was impossible, because saying a problem was impossible from across a thin link was a coward's certainty, and he would not give it to her.
I'm coming. A couple of hours. No more.
Come now. As soon as you can. Not at midnight.
Eli?
The shop was quiet after.

Far off, a transformer he could not see held a single low note, the sound of power moving, of something still being delivered to someone. The call window had collapsed. What was left on it was the notice she had read him, the three machines in plain type, and the time beside them, 23:59, a little under twelve hours away.

He did not move for a while. Under all of it, breathing on a controller that would lose its yes at midnight, a sixty-one-year-old man with a name Lena was keeping back. He looked at the three machines, and the single time beneath them, and there was no way back and no way around it. This was the one he could not fix.
